auto_merge.commit_message:
Problem:
Use vim.lsp.util.apply_text_edits to re-apply the same textedit causes
an incorrect edit, because apply_text_edits silently modifies the
parameter.

Solution:
- Avoid changing `text_edit._index`.
- Document this fun feature.

Helped-by: Riley Bruins <ribru17@hotmail.com>
Helped-by: Yi Ming <ofseed@foxmail.com>
Co-authored-by: Justin M. Keyes <justinkz@gmail.com>
auto_merge.commit_title: fix(lsp): malformed edit if apply_text_edits() is called twice #34954

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

comment_data.body: Maybe a comment here would be helpful
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -321,7 +321,9 @@ function M.apply_text_edits(text_edits, bufnr, position_encoding, change_annotat
     -- Fix reversed range and indexing each text_edits
     for index, text_edit in ipairs(text_edits) do
       --- @cast text_edit lsp.TextEdit|{_index: integer}
-      text_edit._index = index
+      if text_edit._index == nil then

comment_data.body: Maybe `text_edits = vim.tbl_extend("force", {} ,text_edits)` or `text_edits = vim.deepcopy(text_edits)` or another way to shallow/deep copy at the top of the parent function would be more clear?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -321,7 +321,9 @@ function M.apply_text_edits(text_edits, bufnr, position_encoding, change_annotat
     -- Fix reversed range and indexing each text_edits
     for index, text_edit in ipairs(text_edits) do
       --- @cast text_edit lsp.TextEdit|{_index: integer}
-      text_edit._index = index
+      if text_edit._index == nil then

comment_data.body: That could be expensive, especially if a lot of edits come in from a formatter eg. I think if deep copying is needed, it should be done in the plugin side. In fact, maybe a separate doc PR would be nice to explicitly say that this function modifies the table of edits?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -321,7 +321,9 @@ function M.apply_text_edits(text_edits, bufnr, position_encoding, change_annotat
     -- Fix reversed range and indexing each text_edits
     for index, text_edit in ipairs(text_edits) do
       --- @cast text_edit lsp.TextEdit|{_index: integer}
-      text_edit._index = index
+      if text_edit._index == nil then

comment_data.body:
> In fact, maybe a separate doc PR would be nice to explicitly say that this function modifies the table of edits?

Yep, we can wait for another review and then decide what to do with that.

> That could be expensive, especially if a lot of edits come in from a formatter eg

Yeah, deepcopy is indeed can be expensive, but what about shallow copy like vim.tbl_extend, it will modify edits with indexes, but will sort only new object instead of provided one. I don't think it is expensive cause it only creates a new parent and fills it with existing children. For example formatting `eval.c` contains 1355 text edits with my config and `text_edits = vim.tbl_extend("force", {}, text_edits)` takes less than 1ms, `table.sort` is way more expensive.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -321,7 +321,9 @@ function M.apply_text_edits(text_edits, bufnr, position_encoding, change_annotat
     -- Fix reversed range and indexing each text_edits
     for index, text_edit in ipairs(text_edits) do
       --- @cast text_edit lsp.TextEdit|{_index: integer}
-      text_edit._index = index
+      if text_edit._index == nil then

comment_regular_data.body: Thanks for the thorough explanation. Just curious, what is the use case of storing text edits and potentially applying the same ones multiple times?

> Thanks for the thorough explanation. Just curious, what is the use case of storing text edits and potentially applying the same ones multiple times?

My use case is code actions, `lspsaga` and `fzf-lua`(only if a last element does not have comma at eol) are affected by this, `tiny-code-action.nvim` is fine though.
  
The issue because of preview implementation:
1. Request all code actions  - 1 time and provide them into callbacks(this is why sort in place affects them)
2. Generate preview(they use `apply_text_edits` on tmp buffer and then `vim.diff` to generate diff content) - first call of `vim.lsp.util.apply_text_edits`, sort in place + indexes for text_edits fetched in step 1.
3. User picks this code action - second call of `vim.lsp.util.apply_text_edits` with `text_edits` fetched in step 1 and modified in step 2 by preview implementation, wrong applying order if there are multiple edits per code action.

I created the issue #39344 that seems to be solved by this PR.

I want to share the diff with the test-reproducer (using diff I faced problems with) and the fix: 

```diff
diff --git a/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/util.lua b/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/util.lua
index 9231976c58..b4dc06fde4 100644
--- a/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/util.lua
+++ b/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/util.lua
@@ -317,29 +317,36 @@ function M.apply_text_edits(text_edits, bufnr, position_encoding, change_annotat
   local has_eol_text_edit = false
 
   local function apply_text_edits()
-    -- Fix reversed range and indexing each text_edits
+    local normalized_edits = {}
+
+    -- Fix reversed range and index each text edit without mutating the caller's table.
     for index, text_edit in ipairs(text_edits) do
-      --- @cast text_edit lsp.TextEdit|{_index: integer}
-      text_edit._index = index
+      local start_range = text_edit.range.start
+      local end_range = text_edit.range['end']
 
       if
-        text_edit.range.start.line > text_edit.range['end'].line
-        or text_edit.range.start.line == text_edit.range['end'].line
-          and text_edit.range.start.character > text_edit.range['end'].character
+        start_range.line > end_range.line
+        or start_range.line == end_range.line and start_range.character > end_range.character
       then
-        local start = text_edit.range.start
-        text_edit.range.start = text_edit.range['end']
-        text_edit.range['end'] = start
+        start_range, end_range = end_range, start_range
       end
+
+      local new_text = string.gsub(text_edit.newText, '\r\n?', '\n')
+      normalized_edits[index] = {
+        range = {
+          start = start_range,
+          ['end'] = end_range,
+        },
+        newText = new_text,
+        _index = index,
+      }
     end
 
-    --- @cast text_edits (lsp.TextEdit|lsp.AnnotatedTextEdit|{_index: integer})[]
-
-    -- Sort text_edits
-    ---@param a (lsp.TextEdit|lsp.AnnotatedTextEdit|{_index: integer})
-    ---@param b (lsp.TextEdit|lsp.AnnotatedTextEdit|{_index: integer})
+    -- Sort text edits
+    ---@param a lsp.TextEdit|{_index: integer}
+    ---@param b lsp.TextEdit|{_index: integer}
     ---@return boolean
-    table.sort(text_edits, function(a, b)
+    table.sort(normalized_edits, function(a, b)
       if a.range.start.line ~= b.range.start.line then
         return a.range.start.line > b.range.start.line
       end
@@ -356,10 +363,7 @@ function M.apply_text_edits(text_edits, bufnr, position_encoding, change_annotat
       end
     end
 
-    for _, text_edit in ipairs(text_edits) do
-      -- Normalize line ending
-      text_edit.newText, _ = string.gsub(text_edit.newText, '\r\n?', '\n')
-
+    for _, text_edit in ipairs(normalized_edits) do
       -- Convert from LSP style ranges to Neovim style ranges.
       local start_row = text_edit.range.start.line
       local start_col = get_line_byte_from_position(bufnr, text_edit.range.start, position_encoding)
diff --git a/test/functional/plugin/lsp_spec.lua b/test/functional/plugin/lsp_spec.lua
index 84eb63beea..1f8e3a4d33 100644
--- a/test/functional/plugin/lsp_spec.lua
+++ b/test/functional/plugin/lsp_spec.lua
@@ -2244,6 +2244,57 @@ describe('LSP', function()
       }, buf_lines(1))
     end)
 
+    it('can apply the same text edit list to multiple buffers', function()
+      local result = exec_lua(function()
+        local function edit(start_col, end_col, new_text)
+          return {
+            range = {
+              start = { line = 0, character = start_col },
+              ['end'] = { line = 0, character = end_col },
+            },
+            newText = new_text,
+          }
+        end
+
+        local original_lines = {
+          'if strings.HasSuffix(name, suffix) {',
+          '\treturn strings.TrimSuffix(name, suffix)',
+          '}',
+        }
+
+        local text_edits = {
+          edit(3, 3, 'before, ok := '),
+          edit(3, 20, 'strings.CutSuffix'),
+        }
+        local before = vim.deepcopy(text_edits)
+
+        local function apply_to_new_buffer()
+          local bufnr = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true)
+          vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(bufnr, 0, -1, false, original_lines)
+          vim.lsp.util.apply_text_edits(text_edits, bufnr, 'utf-16')
+          local lines = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines(bufnr, 0, -1, false)
+          vim.api.nvim_buf_delete(bufnr, { force = true })
+          return lines
+        end
+
+        local first = apply_to_new_buffer()
+        local after_first = vim.deepcopy(text_edits)
+        local second = apply_to_new_buffer()
+
+        return {
+          before = before,
+          after_first = after_first,
+          after_second = text_edits,
+          first = first,
+          second = second,
+        }
+      end)
+
+      eq(result.first, result.second)
+      eq(result.before, result.after_first)
+      eq(result.before, result.after_second)
+    end)
+
     it('it restores marks', function()
       eq(true, api.nvim_buf_set_mark(1, 'a', 2, 1, {}))
       apply_text_edits({
```

Hope it helps!
